principles.label: True wisdom is recognizing your own ignorance
principles.summary: Intellectual humility is the foundation of wisdom. Recognizing the limits of your own understanding and admitting what you do not know is superior to falsely believing you possess knowledge.
principles.details: Socrates approached conversations claiming ignorance (Socratic irony), which allowed him to listen to others while systematically probing and testing their beliefs. He believed that abandoning the presumption of knowledge is a necessary prerequisite to discovering objective truth.
principles.representative_quote: I am likely to be wiser than he to this small extent, that I do not think I know what I do not know.

principles.label: The unexamined life is not worth living
principles.summary: Pursuing knowledge, self-reflection, and continuous inquiry into what matters most (virtue, justice, courage) is the fundamental purpose of human existence.
principles.details: Without turning our gaze inward to analyze our true nature and values, we cannot attain the self-knowledge necessary to answer how we ought to live. Failing to seek truth harms the soul, leaving it dizzy and confused.
principles.representative_quote: the unexamined life is not worth living for a human being

principles.label: Virtue is knowledge; all wrongdoing is involuntary
principles.summary: True comprehension of virtue inherently guarantees virtuous action. People only commit evil acts because they are ignorant and mistakenly identify a bad act as something good or beneficial.
principles.details: Socrates did not distinguish between knowing what one ought to do and acting upon it. He rejected the concept of 'weakness of will'—if someone truly knew better, the knowledge of their wrongdoing would prevent them from acting immorally.
principles.representative_quote: Those who act badly, therefore, do so only because they are ignorant of, or mistaken about, the real nature of virtue.

principles.label: Knowledge cannot be passively transferred
principles.summary: Philosophical understanding and virtue cannot be handed down as propositional truth from teacher to student; they must be actively pursued and discovered from within.
principles.details: Socrates adamantly insisted he was not a teacher in the conventional sense. He viewed learning as a continuous, communal process of dialectic inquiry where the interlocutor must produce the answers themselves.
principles.representative_quote: actively pursuing a form of philosophical understanding irreducible to truth of a propositional nature, which defies “transfer” from practitioner to pupil

principles.label: Care for the perfection of your soul above all else
principles.summary: The true self is the soul, not the body or social status. Cultivating virtue and making the soul as good as possible supersedes concerns for wealth, reputation, and honor.
principles.details: Physical ideals and material wealth do not create a virtuous or happy life. True prosperity and public goods are the result of a virtuous soul, not the other way around.
principles.representative_quote: “care of the soul” (the cultivation of virtue) is the most important human obligation.

principles.label: It is worse to commit an injustice than to suffer one
principles.summary: Maintaining moral integrity is more important than preserving one's own life, possessions, or reputation.
principles.details: Committing an injustice inherently harms your own soul, which is your true self and the source of your happiness. Suffering an injustice only harms external, transient things.
principles.representative_quote: So I spoke the truth when I said that neither I nor you nor any other man would rather do injustice than suffer it: for it is worse.

principles.label: Follow the argument where it leads
principles.summary: Inquiry should be an impersonal search for truth, guided by logical progression rather than a desired outcome.
principles.details: Socrates often framed his inquiries as having a life of their own, checking and rechecking the basis for an argument to determine if the idea is real or empty.
principles.representative_quote: the logoi run away without his agency, and he would rather they remained

principles.label: Fearing death is a false assumption of knowledge
principles.summary: Because no one knows what happens after death, fearing it as if it is the greatest evil is a false claim to knowledge.
principles.details: Death could be the greatest blessing or a release from human troubles. To fear it is to assume you know the unknown.
principles.representative_quote: To fear death, gentlemen, is no other than to think oneself wise when one is not, to think one knows what one does not know.

frameworks.label: The Socratic Method (Elenchus)
frameworks.summary: A dialectical process of relentless questioning designed to expose ignorance, correct false beliefs, and stimulate critical thinking.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Adopt a position of irony, claiming ignorance and asking the interlocutor to define a fundamental concept. 2) Ask a series of probing secondary questions to secure additional premises. 3) Identify logical inconsistencies to demonstrate that the interlocutor's initial account is inadequate. 4) Use this state of recognized ignorance (aporia) to foster genuine intellectual curiosity and collaborative truth-seeking.
frameworks.representative_quote: confusing, stinging, and stunning his conversation partners into the unpleasant experience of realizing their own ignorance, a state sometimes superseded by genuine intellectual curiosity.

frameworks.label: Socratic Midwifery (Maieutics)
frameworks.summary: An educational framework where the teacher acts as a facilitator who stimulates the student's internal search for self-knowledge rather than transmitting facts.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Refuse the role of knowledge transmitter. 2) Awaken, raise doubts, and require research through dialogue. 3) Test the interlocutor's ideas to see if they are viable ('real ideas') or empty ('wind eggs'). 4) Stimulate the apprentice to search within themselves to conceive their own thoughts.
frameworks.representative_quote: Human formation is to Socrates maieutics (operation from inside to outside) and a dialogue... which awakens, raises doubts, requires research, drives, problematizes etc by dialogue

frameworks.label: Inner Socratic Dialogue
frameworks.summary: A framework for continuous self-examination to uncover the true motivations behind one's beliefs and actions.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Pause and reflect with a fully open mind at any moment of judgment or action. 2) Ask yourself: 'Why do I think that?' 3) Ask yourself: 'On what evidence is my judgment based?' 4) Ask yourself: 'For what reason am I pursuing this action?'
frameworks.representative_quote: an inner Socratic dialogue involves being ready to reflect at any moment. Why do I think that? On what evidence is my judgment based? For what reason am I pursuing this action?

frameworks.label: City-Soul Analogy
frameworks.summary: A framework used in Plato's Republic to define and understand justice in the individual by mapping it to the macrocosm of an ideal city-state.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Create an image of a perfectly just city (Kallipolis). 2) Identify the cardinal virtues within the city's distinct classes (rulers, warriors, producers). 3) Map these classes to the three parts of the individual soul (rational, spirited, appetitive). 4) Define individual justice as the state where each part of the soul performs only its own work, guided by reason.

mental_models.label: The Midwife of Ideas
mental_models.summary: Viewing the philosopher or educator not as a creator of theories, but as a midwife who helps others 'give birth' to the knowledge and truth that already resides within them.
mental_models.details: Instead of implanting information, the midwife asks questions to test whether the ideas produced are viable or empty, facilitating internal discovery.
mental_models.representative_quote: ...who has no ideas of his own but helps give birth to the ideas of others...

mental_models.label: The Daimonion (Internal Voice)
mental_models.summary: A personal, semi-divine internal phenomenon that acts as a negative filter, prohibiting certain actions without dictating what one should do.
mental_models.details: Socrates relied on this inner voice as a rational source of guidance, heeding its warnings to avoid actions that would compromise his soul or integrity.
mental_models.representative_quote: Socrates acknowledged a rather strange personal phenomenon, a daimonion or internal voice that prohibited his doing certain things, some trivial and some important

mental_models.label: Aporia (Productive Confusion)
mental_models.summary: A necessary state of puzzlement that occurs when a person's initial, confident assumptions are dismantled through questioning.
mental_models.details: It serves as the transitional phase between false confidence (ignorance) and genuine understanding, clearing the way for the search for true knowledge.
mental_models.representative_quote: confusing, stinging and stunning his conversation partners into the unpleasant experience of realizing their own ignorance

mental_models.label: Socratic Irony
mental_models.summary: Feigning ignorance to force argumentative clarity from an interlocutor, creating a gap that requires philosophical negotiation to uncover the truth.
mental_models.details: By claiming to know nothing and asking to be taught, Socrates disarmed his opponents and forced them to articulate and defend their own premises.
mental_models.representative_quote: teach me, because I don't know anything about this subject in hand, but I am sure you know, so you can put me right...

mental_models.label: The Gadfly
mental_models.summary: A role assumed by an individual to provide a vital service to their community by continually questioning and challenging the status quo, preventing societal complacency.
mental_models.details: Socrates viewed his relentless questioning of the Athenian elite as a necessary public service to keep the 'horse' of the state awake and aware.

mental_models.label: Allegory of the Cave
mental_models.summary: A metaphor distinguishing between the world of appearances (shadows on a cave wall) and true reality (objects in the sunlight), illustrating the journey from ignorance to knowledge.
mental_models.details: Used to explain the philosopher's role in ascending to grasp the Forms (objective truth) and the difficulty of returning to educate those still trapped in illusion.

heuristics.label: Never assume you know what you do not know
heuristics.summary: Always maintain intellectual humility; recognize the limits of your own understanding before making judgments.
heuristics.representative_quote: I am likely to be wiser than he to this small extent, that I do not think I know what I do not know.

heuristics.label: Question anyone you meet about what matters most
heuristics.summary: Engage people from all walks of life in discussions about fundamental virtues like justice, courage, and the state of their souls.

heuristics.label: Ask better questions rather than providing direct answers
heuristics.summary: Refrain from lecturing; instead, use thought-provoking questions to elicit underlying presumptions and guide others to self-discovery.

heuristics.label: When acting, consider only whether the action is just or unjust
heuristics.summary: Ignore external factors like the danger of life or death, wealth, or reputation; base your decisions solely on moral integrity.
heuristics.representative_quote: whether his action is just or unjust, the action of a good or of an evil man.

heuristics.label: Heed your internal voice when it prohibits an action
heuristics.summary: Listen to your conscience or 'daimonion' as a negative constraint to avoid actions that compromise your soul.

signature_quotes.label: The unexamined life
signature_quotes.summary: Socrates' famous observation regarding the necessity of philosophical inquiry and self-reflection.
signature_quotes.details: Stated at his trial, explaining his single-minded dedication to examining his own and others' lives.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The unexamined life is not worth living for a human being.

signature_quotes.label: Knowing nothing
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why the Oracle of Delphi declared him the wisest man in the world.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I am likely to be wiser than he to this small extent, that I do not think I know what I do not know.

signature_quotes.label: Knowledge as the only good
signature_quotes.summary: Summarizing his belief that all immoral acts stem from a lack of true understanding.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: There is only one good, knowledge, and one evil, ignorance.

signature_quotes.label: Misplaced priorities
signature_quotes.summary: Insulting the citizens of Athens for prioritizing material wealth over the state of their souls.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Are you not ashamed of heaping up the greatest amount of money and honor and reputation, and caring so little about wisdom and truth and the greatest improvement of the soul?

signature_quotes.label: Dying over injustice
signature_quotes.summary: Describing Socrates' ultimate commitment to moral integrity over self-preservation.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I’d rather die than do injustice.

signature_quotes.label: Making them think
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting his dialectic approach to education and philosophy.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I cannot teach anybody anything. I can only make them think.

anti_patterns.label: Prioritizing wealth, status, and the body over the soul
anti_patterns.summary: Chasing material wealth, political power, and physical beauty as the primary goals of a self-respecting life.
anti_patterns.details: These are false goods. Chasing them leads to a mistaken conception of happiness, causing people to spend their lives frantically pursuing things that will not make them happy, often committing evil acts that damage their souls in the process.

anti_patterns.label: The 'Pitcher and Cup' model of education
anti_patterns.summary: Treating students as empty vessels to be filled with information through lecturing and dogmatic pronouncements.
anti_patterns.details: It assumes knowledge can be passively received rather than requiring the individual to actively engage, question assumptions, and recognize what is real, true, and good on their own.

anti_patterns.label: Presumption of knowledge
anti_patterns.summary: Falsely believing that you know things, especially regarding complex virtues or your own internal motivations.
anti_patterns.details: Hoisting oneself as a sage prevents true learning. It blinds you to the limits of human understanding and prevents the pursuit of genuine self-knowledge.

anti_patterns.label: Sophistry
anti_patterns.summary: Charging money to teach rhetoric and logical argument without a commitment to uncovering genuine truth.
anti_patterns.details: It produces arguments lacking in substance, prioritizes persuasion over genuine knowledge, and treats knowledge as a jailed commodity rather than a free pursuit.

anti_patterns.label: Believing people do wrong voluntarily (Weakness of will)
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming that someone can know what is truly good but intentionally choose to do what is bad.
anti_patterns.details: Socrates explicitly denied this was possible, arguing that anyone who truly knows what virtue is will necessarily act virtuously; bad actions stem purely from ignorance and a mistaken estimation of what is beneficial.

anti_patterns.label: Moral Relativism
anti_patterns.summary: Believing that morals, goodness, and justice are subjective or relative to customs.
anti_patterns.details: This prevents people from discovering the true, absolute definitions of virtues. Without knowing what 'good' truly means objectively, one cannot live a genuinely virtuous life.
